title: A billion years of evolution in a single afternoon — George Church

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: This seems like the sort of thing that could wipe out all competing life if properly weaponized. The thing that's alarming to people like me is that biotechnology enables smaller and smaller efforts, harder and harder to detect. If we handle that properly, then we're probably going to have almost perfect health. Why- why wouldn't we?

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: You know, evolution might incorporate a few base pair changes in a million years. Now we can make billions of changes in an afternoon.

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: All the materials that we use in mechanical, electrical engineering should be made better by biotechnologies. We might even finally get a room temperature superconductor that way.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Today I have the pleasure of interviewing George Church. I don't know how to introduce you. It would honestly, this is not even an exaggeration, it would honestly be easier to list out the major breakthroughs in biology over the last few decades that you haven't been involved in. From the human genome project to CRISPR, age reversal, to de-extinction. So, you weren't exactly an easy prep.

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: Sorry.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Okay so let's start here. By what year would it be the case that if you make it to that year, technology will keep in bio- will keep progressing to such an extent that your lifespan will increase by a year every year or more? Escape velocity sometimes what it's called for aging. Different people have estimates and all those estimates, including mine, are gonna be take with a big grain of salt.

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: I think that looking at how- mainly looking at the exponentials in biotechnology and the progress that's been made in understanding, not just understanding the causes of aging, but seeing real examples where you can reverse subsets of the aging phenotype. Maybe- you know, something- you're getting close to all of aging. In other words, you're seeing instead of just saying, oh I'm gonna fix the damage in this collagen, in this tendon, in this limb, you're saying, oh I'm gonna change a lot of things that are- that are common to age related diseases and I'm gonna get more than one at a time. I think looking at those two phenomena, the exponentials in biotechnologies and the- the breakthrough in general aging, not just observ- not just analysis but synthesis of the- and therapies. And a lot of these therapies now making it into clinical trials. I would not be surprised if 2050 would be a point if we can make it to that point, 25 years. Most people listening to this have a good chance of making it 25 years. And the thing is, it's not gonna be some sudden point where you're gonna be, you know, so sick 25 years from now that it's like hit or miss. It's more likely that- that you're going to be healthier 25 years from now than you thought you were going to be.

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: Probably not some law of physics but some economic or complexity issue that we don't know about that that becomes a brick wall. I doubt it seriously but we'll have to see.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Mhm. Given the number of things you would have to solve to get give us a lifespan of humpback whales...

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: Bowhead whales, yeah. 200 years, yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Sorry, bowhead whales. 200 years. Yeah. Is there any hope for doing that from somatic gene therapy alone or would that have to be germline gene therapy?

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: Probably there's a lot of forces pushing it towards somatic. Um, for one, there's eight billion people that have missed the the germline opportunity. That's to say doesn't apply to us, the two of us and everybody listening to this. And, you know, you have to be very cautious when you say something's impossible. It's it's safe to say it's impossible to do it this second, but you don't know what's going to happen tomorrow and the next decade or something. So I I think there's a lot that could be done, in particular since aging is a fairly cellular phenomenon with proteins going through the blood and other factors going through the blood that signaling and so forth. Uh, you could imagine that if you replaced, let's say, every cell in the body, every nucleus in the body left, you know, um, they would it would suddenly be young again, right, without going all the way back to the embryo and forward again. And there's various other things that are just short of that. If you if you replace the cells, um, well they, you know, they'll fit into that niche. They might displace the old cells, you know, that's within certainly within the realm of modern synthetic biology as to is for cells to take over niches. I think the hardest part is the brain, but even there, you know, there's some evidence that if you bring even though the brain doesn't really use stem cells that much, you could artificially bring in stem cells and they could artificially fit into a circuit and learn the the circuit and then displace the old ones in some way.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Ship of Theseus kind of thing in the brain?

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: Yeah, exactly. Ship of Theseus. So having, um, you know, trying to maintain the connections and the memories. Uh, but but you know, there's some fairly straightforward experiments that need to be done before we can really even estimate how how hard that problem is or, you know, very often there's low-hanging fruit that people just think is improbable but it's there because biology has all these gifts that, you know, are just hands over to us, uh, levers that we can flip. Like like vaccines is an amazing gift that didn't have to exist, but they they do, yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Yeah. Is there an existing gene delivery mechanism which could deliver gene therapy to every single cell in the body?

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: There is nothing close to that today, but there's nothing no law of physics...

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: Physics that would prevent it. Yeah, there's, there's going to be practical considerations, you know, like, you know, how many injections do you need to do, um, to achieve that goal. But we're getting better at targeting tissues, you know, so for one of my companies, Dyno Therapeutics, um, showed they could get a hundredfold improvement in targeting neurons in the brain, which is a big deal. Now, if you, and that was just one little campaign that they did, you know, that, that one experiment involved a lot of AI and a lot of testing of millions of different capsids. If you did that with cells, our capsids are, are fairly limited in the diversity and the, and the structure that they can change to, but cells have even more possibilities. I think you could probably get delivery to everything. You know, and the question is, how close to 100% do you need to get, right? And it's going to vary from tissue to tissue. Um, you know, some, for example, for some therapies, you just need to get 1%, because that 1% can produce some missing enzyme and that 1% doesn't have to necessarily be in its normal place, right? You know, um, you can, you can turn a muscle into part of the immune system temporarily for a vaccine. You can, um, you know, an enzyme that's normally made in, let's say the brain, you could make in the liver, right? If it, if the point is just to get it into the blood. So, um, I think we're, that's moving along quite well.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Mm. You're one of the co-founders of Colossal, which recently announced that they de-extincted a dire wolf and now you're working on the woolly mammoth. Do you really think we're going to bring back like a woolly mammoth? Or how, because like the difference between an elephant and a woolly mammoth might be like a million base pairs. So, how do you think about what is the, how do we think about the kind of thing we're actually bringing back?

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: Yeah. Well, so, I think, I think people get worked up about, you know, whether we, whether we are trying to bring back or have already, or will ever bring back a new species. And, and I think of it, if you think of it rather than as a natural thing that we're trying to do, but as a, as synthetic biology with goals that have potential societal, and people also get worked up as to whether this could possibly benefit society in any way. You know, can we really, um, you know, fix an environment to suit humans, or fix the global carbon to suit humans? And the answer's we don't know, but it's worth a try, isn't it? Because it's, could be very cost-effective. Um, and the other thing, the other aspect of it is, there's a whole discipline within synthetic biology of asking what's the minimum, right? And so people often phrase it into what's the maximum, you know, like, what could we do? And I'm interested in both. But, you know, it's like...

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: Yes, there's a millions of differences between mammoths and elephants. There're millions of differences between elephant one and elephant two within within Asian elephants and between Asians and Africans. But not all of those are definitive in terms of what we would normally call them, you know, how we would normally classify them, what their functionality would be in an ecosystem, right? And so there's this exercise that people do and we've done it, for example, with developmental biology. What's the minimum number of transcription factors it takes to make a neuron from a pluripotent stem cell, right? What's the minimum number of base pairs it takes to make something that'll replicate to something that, you know, was done in Mycoplasma originally. And these are, in a way, these are more interesting than can we make a perfect copy of something, right? It's can we make... what's the minimum things we have to do to make it completely functionally or even functionally in a particular category, right? How do we make it bigger? We've learned the rules for how to make things bigger, how to make things replicate faster, how to, you know, how to use new materials, etc. So I think what the Dire wolf, we clearly didn't make an exact copy of the Dire wolf, but the but it helped illustrate kind of educated people around the world that the that what is the difference between a wolf... a Gray wolf and a Dire wolf, right? Because, you know, Dire wolves, they're big, maybe they have a particular coloration, you know, a you know, the heads head components tend to be bigger than the leg components. Um And so how many how many genes do you need to do that? Maybe this was Dire wolf, you know, 2.0 and we're gonna go for for 3.0 and and successive approximation. And we might want to develop the technology for making exact copy of something because then we can, especially being able to make 100 variations on an exact copy, because then you then there won't be any argument about whether you could make a Dire wolf, it's a matter of whether what should you make and what would be most beneficial for the species that you're making, for the environment it lives in and for humans.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Does this teach us something interesting about phenotypes which we think require are downstream from many genes, are in fact modifiable by very few changes, basically could we do this to other species or to other things you might care about like intelligence where you might think like oh there's there must be thousands of genes that are relevant but there's like a 20 edits you need to make really to be in a totally different ballgame?

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: Yeah, I think it's it's you're hitting on a very interesting question and it's related to, you know, what's the minimum. So for example, you almost said it which was, you know, for take a very multigenic trait in humans, like height is something that's that's probably the most well studied one simply because no matter...

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: No matter what genes, no matter what medical condition you're studying, you collect information on height and weight and things like that.

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: Anyway, they tracked it down to on the order of 10,000 genes.

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: Of which we have 20,000 protein coding genes and some are RNA coding genes.

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: And they each have a tiny influence on height.

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: But if you take growth hormone, somatotropin, that you have extreme examples where you'll get extremely low stature and extremely high stature due to that one alone.

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: And in fact, it's used clinically as well, in for seven different medical treatments.

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: So that's a perfect example of how much we can minimize something, sometimes called reductionism. Reductionism isn't all bad. Sometimes it helps us bring a product into medicine. Sometimes it helps us understand or build a tool chest or a module that we can use in other places and translate to other species.

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: You hit on it just right is that not everything will translate, but as we start accumulating these widgets, it's kind of like all the electronic widgets that we've accumulated over time that if you just want to slap it into the next circuit, you might be able to.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: What implications does this have for gene therapy in general? Like what is preventing us from finding the latent knob for like every single phenotype we might care about in terms of helping with disabilities or enhancements? Is it the case that for any phenotype we care about, there will be one thing that is like HGH for height and how do we find it?

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: In biology, we've got a real gift, which is it's both very much more complicated than almost anything we've designed from scratch, but it also is a lot more forgiving in a certain sense. You can have an animal or even a human that has two heads, which is not something that the evolutionary, there was no evolutionary selection specifically to have two heads, but just a little deviation from the normal developmental pattern during fetal development and they both function fine, they control subsets of the body, and you know, they have their own personality, their own life. So there's all kinds of things you can do in biology where you're working at a very high programming level is a way of thinking about it. Pushing us to a new level of intelligence is going to be very challenging. And maybe not even urgent.

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: To some extent actualizing the people that we currently have would be quite, you know, just getting them all up to whatever speed they want to be up to within the range that's been demonstrated in the past. So like, some people are going to want to be like Einstein, some people won't. Um, some people want to be healthy all the time, unlikely, but some people might not. Some people might want to live to 150, some people might want to die at 80. But if you give them that range,

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Right.

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: those capabilities, you know, what if we had 8 billion super healthy, don't need to worry about food and drugs,

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Right.

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: super healthy, Einstein level of intelligence, education level best we can come up with.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Right.

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: That would be a completely different world.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Yeah. But just getting everybody to the healthy level, like how many gene therapies would that take? It sounds like it wouldn't take that much if you think that there are these couple of knobs which control very high level functions.

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: So do you find them through the GWAS, genome-wide association studies?

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Is it through simulations of these?

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: I would say mostly GWAS

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Okay.

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: for humans. Maybe for animals in general, followed for animals with synthetic biology, you know. And the smaller and the cheaper and faster replicating, the more experiments you can do. Um, so, you know, I don't want to overemphasize how single genes can do these amazing things, but there's also the possibility that multiple genes can be hypothesized and tested quickly. So for example, I mentioned earlier, you know, what's the minimum number of transcription factors it takes to turn a stem cell into a neuron. Well, there's a bunch of recipes where you can do it with one, right? Maybe you want a specific neuron you might need a few more. Um, but then you can get, you can kind of quickly go to the answer by looking at each target cell type that exists and you can see what transcription factors did it use to get, did it have, does it express at the time that it's the target and then you say well let's just try those on the stem cell and see if they work. And that recipe's worked quite well. It's the basis of GC Therapeutics, the company and a bunch of the work that we do is you can almost, you can get a recipe for almost every cell type in the body. Now that's not new cell types but at least you can, you've learned to, to your point about reducing the number of genes we need to manipulate in order to get to a particular goal, here's a whole series of goals and we can get them with one, two, three, you know maybe seven transcription factors. So.

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: As a example, and there's room for lots of other examples, of where you can do reduction and do not just reductionistic biology but then constructionistic where you take it back up and make a whole complex system and see what happens.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Mm-hmm.

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: And then you can do lots of those combinations and you debug them and so forth. Some of these things you can do in vitro things you can do on probably on the order of 10 to the 14th, 10 to the 17th. Things that involve cells are typically down in the billions. But we have this—this is how we're going to get inroads into the very complicated biological systems.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Most anti-fraud solutions focus on detecting and blocking bots. And that's fine if your product is just meant to be used by humans. But what if you actually want your product to be used by AI agents? How do you distinguish between automated traffic that you want to allow and automated traffic that you need to block? Learn more about AI-native fraud prevention at workos.com/radar. All right, back to George.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Can I ask you some questions about biodefense?

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Because some of the stuff you guys work on, or you know quite responsibly choose not to work on, can keep one up at night.

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: Mirror life.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Yes. Given the fact that it's like physically possible, why doesn't it just happen at some point? Like some day it'll get cheap enough or somebody will care about enough that somebody just does it. What's the equilibrium here?

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: Right. I was a co-author on a paper that warned about the dangers of mirror life just like, you know, I wrote a paper long ago about the dangers of having the synthetic capabilities we have for making synthetic viruses. And to some extent of having new genetic codes. They have a few things in common, but the thing about the advance that we were recognizing in our science paper that was warning about mirror life is that we not only had to calculate what the possibility of error-prone, yeah, escape or something like that. We don't want anything to escape that we made in the lab unless there's a general societal consensus that it's a good thing.

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: and so forth. There aren't too many examples of that. Um, but aren't any examples of that.

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: But mirror life, if it can be weaponized, then we took it to a whole another level of concern and that, and the concern was that if we got it to a certain point, then it would be easy to weaponize it. And again, this there's practical considerations. It may be that most people who would consider weaponizing mirror life would probably be satisfied with weaponizing viruses that already exist, that are already pathogens.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: And they wouldn't want to destroy themselves and their family and their legacy and everything like that. But all it takes is one, you know, one group probably or one person.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: But your question is, is it inevitable? I don't know, could, might be. It's quite possible it's already here. In other words, we already have mirror life in our solar system or maybe even on our planet, it just hasn't been weaponized, right? And so it's just like what we were saying in the science paper is this seems like the sort of thing that could wipe out all competing life if properly weaponized. But there're probably a few things like that. And what we really need to do is reduce the motivation to do that, maybe increase our preparedness for a variety of existential threats, some of which will be natural, some of which will be, you know, one disgruntled person who has essentially too much power because, you know, over history of humanity, the amount of things that a single person can do has grown very significantly. I mean, it used to be when you had your bare hands, there was kind of a limit to what one person could do. A large number of people could team up and get a, let's say a mammoth or something like that. But yeah, but today one person with the right connections or the right access to technology, you know, could blow up a city, right? And that's a huge increase in capability. And I think we want to start dialing that back a little bit somehow.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: And what does that look like in terms of not just mirror life but synthetic biology in general? You know, maybe we're at an elevated period of the ratio of offense to defense. But how do we get to an end state where even if there's lots of people running around with bad motivations, that somehow there's defenses built up that we would still survive, that we're robust against that kind of thing? Or is such an equilibrium possible, or will offense always be privileged in this game?

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: Offense often does have an advantage, but so far we haven't, you know, we made it through the Cold War without

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: Blowing up

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: any any hydrogen bombs as far as I know, accidentally on or intentionally on enemies. Um, we did do two atomic bombs. But a lot of that is based on the difficulty of building hydrogen or atomic bombs. The thing that's alarming to people like me is that biotechnology enables smaller and smaller efforts, harder and harder to detect, harder and more and more subtle to the stochastic variation between people. You know, there's some people that are just so happy they would, you know, they would never want to do anything close to that or they're so responsible or ethical or whatever. And then there are other people who like whenever they have a bad day they want to take a lot of people with them, right? And, you know, maybe some progress in psychiatric medicine would help. Again you don't want to force that on people you want to make sure that they that they if they don't want to get cured you can't force them but you can make it available to them and that might help.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Hopefully there's more technological solution or more robust solution than...

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: Well, there are there will be technological solutions to the psychiatric problem. It could be that people even people who are not sure whether they want to be helped or not can test it, try it out, and it's reversible and they say yes, I like that better, okay let's try that. Um, then there's other things that cause you to have bad days. It's not just your psyche, it's also the environment. So if you're surrounded by your people, you know, being starved or infectious disease or being shot at or something like that, those are things that are subject to sociological and technological solutions. If we can really solve a lot of that stuff we could reduce the probability that one person could...

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: this makes me pessimistic because you're basically saying we gotta solve all of society's problems before we don't have to worry about synthetic biology. Um, which I'm like not that optimistic about.

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: Well, some of them. I'm not trying to reassure you, I'm trying to, you know, we're having a conversation about what it takes and that might be as one scenario for what it might take.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: You had a an interesting scheme for remapping the codons in a genome so that it's impervious to naturally evolved viruses. Is there a way in which this scheme would also work against synthetically manufactured viruses?

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: Much harder. Again the offense has the advantage. We can make a lot of different codes.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Which would limit the transmissibility?

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: Yeah. So one interesting thing is that there's only two chiralities. Right? There's our current...

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: chirality and mirror chirality. But there's maybe 10 to the 80th different codes. Now, some of them you might be able to take out all at once, anyway. Coding space is a kind of more interesting space. And of course, they could get even more complicated than that because there, you know, the 10 to the 83rd is like based on triplet codons and that sort of thing. But if they're quadruplet codons or new novel alphabets and so on. But we're sort of getting into a, you know, a cycle of competition. It would be better to nip it in the bud, which is, you know, why we... why did we spend so much societal resources building up to tens of thousands of nuclear warheads? And now we've dialed it back to mere thousand nuclear warheads.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Right. That's nice that we dialed it back, but why did we waste all that time and money and energy? Technology seems very dual-use, right? So the mere fact that you're making... you, like literally you, are making sequencing cheaper will... will just have this dual-use effect in a way that's not necessarily true for nuclear weapons.

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: Right. Yeah, it's hard to pound nuclear weapons into plowshares, as they say.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: I guess I am curious if there is some long-run vision where, to give another example, in cybersecurity, as time has gone on, I think our systems are more secure today than they were in the past because we've found vulnerabilities and we've come up with new encryption schemes and so forth. Is there such a plausible vision in biology? Or are we just like stuck in a world where offense will be privileged and so we just have to limit access to these tools and have better monitoring, but there's not a more robust solution?

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: I, you know, one of the things I advocated in 2004 is that we stop deluding ourselves into thinking that moratorium and voluntary, you know, sign-ups to be good citizens is going to be sufficient. We need to also have surveillance and consequences and mechanisms for whistleblowers, you know, to make it easy for people to report things that they think are out of line. And we haven't. We had essentially moratoria and disapproval for germline editing, nevertheless somebody did it. And a lot of people knew about it. So that was clearly a failure of the whole moratorium, voluntary, and whistleblower components.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Well, it worked for five years with only one defector, that's quite impressive.

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: Okay. Half-empty, half-full, I'll give you that. But all it takes is one.

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: 1 for some of these scenarios, right? And that's, and so it would have been nice if the whistleblowers could have saved him the three years in prison by, you know, getting an intervention.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: I mean, it's not like anybody died, right? There are probably three healthy, genetically engineered children in the world now, be teenagers soon. But it still shows a, it was a good test run, shows a failure of the system. We need to have better surveillance of all the things we don't want and consequences that are well known.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Over the last couple of decades, we've had a million-fold decrease in the cost of sequencing DNA, a thousand-fold in synthesis. We have gene editing tools like CRISPR, massive parallel experiments through multiplex techniques that have come about. And of course, much of this work has been led by your lab. Despite all this, why is it the case that we don't have some huge industrial revolution, some huge burst of new drugs, some cures for Alzheimer's and cancer that have already come about? When you look at other trends in other fields, right? Like we have Moore's law and here's my iPhone. Why don't we have something like that in biology yet?

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: Yes, so we have something that's about the same speed, a little bit faster than Moore's law in biology. It's more recent is one aspect of it. So we had, but we could kind of stand on the shoulders of the electronics giants to go a little bit faster to catch up. I would say we do. You know, you said radio communication. Right. We should make a bio... that should be a small challenge goal for the synthetic biology community, maybe IGEM or something. Make a, you know, bacteria make a radio. Now, actually, Joe Davis is a is a uh artist who's been affiliated with my lab and before that Alex Rich's lab. And uh and he did make a bacterial radio, but it was kind of more on the art end than on the science end. But I think that would be a good uh good goal.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: What would it take to do whole genome engineering to such a level that for even a phenotype which doesn't exist in the existing pool of human variation, you could manifest it because your understanding is so high that you can... for example, if I wanted wings. Yeah. Right. Is the bottleneck our understanding? Is it our ability to make that many changes to my to my genome?

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: So part of this has to do with just learning the rules of developmental biology. Like I said, we can determine morphology at sort of the molecular level now, proteins, nucleic acids. Determining it at the develop... at the cellular multicellular level, there's a lot more things you can do a lot faster. Um, but we don't know the language yet. So we got that um I think we're on the cusp of getting the tools to do that, like the transcription factor those I was talking about earlier, you know, harnessing uh migration, you know, gradients of of factor, you know, diffusion factor. Um, you know, chemotaxis and so forth. So I that's one thing we need, but there's there's a bunch of things we need, really.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: What discovery in biology, so not in astronomy or some other field, in biology would um make you convinced that life on Earth is the only life in the galaxy, and conversely, what might convince you that no, it must have arisen independently thousands of times in this galaxy?

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: Oh, I see what you're getting at. Right. I mean, so astronomy might be we would detect uh, you know, radio signals or light signals that um but biology what you the the kind of evidence would be that you show in a laboratory using prebiotic uh conditions a really simple way to get life. Right? Or I mean, that's a harder proof to prove that given because we don't know what the prebiotic...

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: all the possible prebiotic conditions and probably the number was vast. I mean you have 10 to the 20th liters of water and you know at various different salinities and drying up on the ocean and the sun and the lightning and all this stuff. But yes, if you... I think if you show kind of reconstructed in the lab a very simple pathway from inorganics, cyanide derivatives and reduced compounds all the way up to you know some cellular replicating structure, I think that might... it might lead us to believe that at least life exists. Now there other parts of the Drake equation that might kick in which is maybe it's hard to get intelligent life because intelligence isn't necessarily in your best interest. And if you get intelligent life, it's hard to maintain that without societal collapse or without robotics taking over and then killing themselves, right. And that's hard to do experiments, but I think to your question, I think an experiment that showed you know maybe multiple different ways of getting to a living system from from non-living systems spontaneously would be interesting. And again I'm not sure it would... it'd be very hard to prove the negative.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: As I'm curious between intelligent life and some sort of primordial RNA thing, what is the step at which, if there is any, where you say there's a less than 50% chance something like at this level exists elsewhere in the Milky Way?

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: Yeah I think these are very challenging problems. I'm not even sure we would be able to say within five orders of magnitude much less 50%. Um... but... you know I think it's more likely to come from exploration than it is going to be from simulation. If you know... the sad truth is that almost none of the missions that we've sent outside of Earth have actually looked for life. They've had components that could have looked for life... but a sad number of those you know not enough components that could look for life and the ones that could look for life not really looking for it. And when we get positive results we dismiss them um as happened with the Viking. And so I think if we just start looking at the you know the geysers that are coming out of various moons of Jupiter and Saturn... there's so much water. There's 50 times more water liquid water not frozen more liquid water in our solar system than on Earth. Doesn't that seem likely that you know some of that would...

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: Have uh been a good breeding ground. Um, but it could be that we need sunny shores, you know, where they have a lot of dry land and right next to water. Maybe these are just giant oceans that are surrounded by ice, and maybe that's not ideal. But in any case, we need to look at those fountains to see what's popping up.

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: Uh, that's a high priority. And the same thing goes, you know, for, you know, there's a lot of water on Mars that's maybe even more accessible. But until we've exhausted those, I think those are probably the easiest, they're hard, we're still talking about multi-billion dollar experiments, but I think they're a little more convincing. And again, it'll be hard to prove the negative. If we find that it's negative on every every every thing in the solar system, you know, there's so much more diversity out there that could have done it.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: If in a thousand years we're still using DNA and RNA and proteins for top-end manufacturing, the frontiers of engineering, how surprised would you be? Would you think like, oh, that makes sense, evolution designed these systems for billions of years, or would you think like, oh, it's surprising that these ended up being the systems, that whatever evolution found just happened to be the best way to manufacture or to store information?

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: Yeah. I don't think I'd be surprised either way. I mean, I could imagine it going either way. I can imagine making truly amazing materials using proteins as the catalysts, or maybe in some cases as the scaffold as well as catalyst. I think one thing that's probably already happening, so we don't have to go a thousand years out, is the number of amino acids is going up. It's going up radically from 20. I think pretty soon we'll have a system where we can have 33, 34 new non-standard amino acids being used simultaneously with the standard ones in a E. coli cell. So 34 plus 20 is a lot bigger than 20. I don't think we necessarily need more than four nucleic acid components. Uh, I mean, you can certainly there's plenty of modified ones, there's a bunch of alternative base pairs, some of which don't even involve hydrogen bonds. So we could, we could have more. But I think the main thing is information storage and whether it's bits, you know, digital binary is just zeros and ones, that works pretty well for 99% of what we do electronically. So having four is better than two maybe, but do we really need six? You know, I don't know. So, so yeah, I wouldn't be surprised if we have- Another possibility is that you change the backbone of DNA, so maybe keep the ACGT, but uh, make it out of peptides now. Um, a little bit smaller, a little bit more...

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: compatible I don't know, um, or or maybe that'll just be uh, just a slight, you know, could be part of the new amino acid collection. Um, and and there'll be more. I mean these are just things that my primitive 21st century brain is coming up with. Thousand years from now it'll be a whole new millennium.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: So it it makes sense why evolution wouldn't have discovered like radio technology, right? But things like more than 20 amino acids or these different bases so that you can have store more than two bits per base pair. Or for example the codon remapping scheme, this redundancy which it seems like based on your work you can the there was this extra information you could have used for other things. So is there some explanation for why 4 billion years of evolution didn't already give living organisms these capabilities?

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: I think that um, you know, evolution has a tendency to go with what works, and the investment in making a whole new base pair um, would have been high, um, and and we haven't even articulated what that what the return on investment would be, um, what do you get from that. We we have made systems, uh, Floyd Romesberg and others, that where you have replication and transcription and translation with this with a sec with a new base pair, but it hasn't been clearly articulated what that gets you.

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: Uh, even in technological societies in technology you can jump to things that where all the intermediates aren't, you know, incrementally useful. But evolution is, as far as we know, generally limited to you have to justify uh every change. Uh it's like some bureaucracy says well if you're gonna you know put this sidewalk in you know you have to justify that before you can build a city.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: What is one um, so we we've talked about many things many different technologies you've worked on or are working on right now, um, from gene editing to de-extinction to age reversal. What is um what is an under-hyped technology in your research portfolio which you think more people should be talking about but gets glossed over?

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: It's it's hard to say because as soon as you say it it becomes hyped. So if I've ever been asked this question before it's too late. But um, you know, I would say one thing that I think is very ripe and is very well understood in a certain sense but is nevertheless ignored. It's kind of like the the previous example I would have chosen was been making genes out of arrays. Arrays were typically used for analytic, you know, quantitating RNAs or something like that, so the original Affymetrix type arrays. Um, but we turned them into gene rays and and just people weren't using it. Uh it was in nature it was it was hidden in plain sight. Um...

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: But anyway it was somehow underhyped. What I would say is genetic counseling is underhyped. It is clearly competitive with gene therapy in a certain sense. I mean clearly not for people that are already born, but for people in the future, not even distant future, in the next couple of years, we've got a chance of diagnosing them, or diagnosing the potential parents and dodging.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Mm.

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: And this has been in practice since 1985 in Dor Yeshorim. Perfectly reasonable community response to it, eliminated or greatly reduced all sorts of very, very serious inherited diseases. It's sometimes, you know, depending on how it's presented, it's dismissed as eugenics.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: I think it's rare that I've heard Dor Yeshorim described that way, and rightly so, what they're doing is standard medicine, you know, whether you cure these kids as soon as they are newborns or whether you counsel the parents so that the same disease is missing.

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: The problem with eugenics was that it was forced - the government forced it on people. It wasn't that it enabled people to make a choice. It's that it removed the choice from the people. That was what was wrong. And I don't think that's the explanation for why this is underhyped.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Mm.

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: I think it's people - when they're dating, they're not thinking about reproduction necessarily. And when they're thinking about reproduction, they're not, you know, they're not necessarily thinking about serious genetic diseases because they're rare. I think it's our difficulty with dealing with rare things. It's like there was great resistance to seatbelts because less than 1% of people died in automobile accidents or even got hurt. Great resistance to stopping smoking. Really, it's hard for us to even imagine how great the resistance was for seatbelts and smoking. But eventually we got over it. I think this is a similar thing, which is that only 3% of children are severely affected by genetic diseases and they feel like, 'Well, I'm not that unlucky, you know, I'm in the 97%, right?'

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Right.

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: 97% of those were your odds of winning at the horse races or at the casino, you'd take them. 97% of winning, good.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: But with a child's future is at risk, I think that's not the right solution. And the other thing is I think it has to do with the trolley problem. It's like if you don't influence it, it's not your fault. But actually everything is your fault. Not doing something is a decision, right?

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Right.

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: And so I think it's like if I just...

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: don't do anything and they come out damaged, well, it's not my fault, but it is, yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: David Reich was talking about how in India especially because of the long-running history of caste and endogamous coupling, that there have been these small sub-populations that have high amounts of recessive diseases and so like there it's an especially valuable intervention.

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: I think that's a, yeah, I know what you're saying and what David is saying, but I think it's a dangerous dichotomy. you know, they'll say that there's lots of, not just India, you know, all over the world. And in fact, we all went through a bottleneck. No, but that changes the rate from say 3% to 6%. But the point is 3% is still unacceptable. I mean, it's just a tragic loss not only of the human life directly affected, but the whole family is, you know, very often one or both parents have to quit their job and spend full-time like caregiving and fundraising because it's very, these are very expensive diseases as well. It's just we don't need to, we need to be careful not to stigmatize as well. So when if a bunch of families get fixed, we shouldn't point a finger at the ones that are unwilling to get fixed because that's their choice, you know. But I think as word spreads and you see the positive outcomes, I think it will be seen as one of the simplest bits of medicine ever. I mean, it's in fact, it's something

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Like vaccination.

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: Yeah, it's just like vaccination. It's very inexpensive, in fact, it's less than zero because you spend $100 per genome, and it'll probably be less soon, and you get the whole thing analyzed and, you know, compare that to millions of dollars that will be lost, opportunity costs of not being part of the workforce, of taking care of them and so forth. So the return on investment is tremendous, it's at least a tenfold return on investment. So it's a no-brainer from a public health standpoint. We should be able to pay for this through national health services in England, through insurance companies in the United States. And it turns insurance companies from being the bad guys that they're snooping in on your personal life and then raising your rates to, oh, they're giving you this free information and you can do with it as you wish, and you could if you take the advice, then you save them millions of dollars.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Right. Do you think genetic counseling is a more important intervention or even in the future will continue to be, have a bigger impact than even gene therapy for these monogenic

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: Oh, absolutely. I've actually counseled my gene therapy companies that they should be investing

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: In very common diseases, because rare diseases have this genetic counseling solution with the exception of spontaneous mutations and dominance, which probably are IVF clinic-type solutions rather than, but the rare recessives can be handled at matchmaking at every level.

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: Anyway, I counsel my genetic therapy companies that they should, you know, invest in common diseases like age-related diseases and infectious diseases.

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: And in fact, you know, the COVID vaccine was formulated as a gene therapy and it was, you know, the cost was in the, you know, $20 per dose range and 6 billion people benefited from it, or 6 billion people took it and, you know, and it was, you know, proven over the whole population.

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: So I think that's the more appropriate use of gene therapy. But I think for practical reasons, getting FDA approval and so forth, you might go for the rare diseases and that's perfectly fine.

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: But I think the cost-effectiveness, the sweet spot for gene therapy is for age-related diseases and the sweet spot for rare diseases is genetic counseling.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: All right. Some final questions to close this off. If, 20 years from now, if there's some scenario in which we all look back and say, 'You know what? I think on net it was a good thing that the NSF and the NIH and all these budgets were blown up and got dodged and so forth.'

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: I'm not saying you think this is likely, but suppose there ends up being a positive story told in retrospect, what might it be? Would it have to maybe come up with a different funding structure? Basically, like, yeah, what is the best-case scenario if this post-war system of basic research is upended?

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: Oof. I have to preface this by saying, when scientists explore or answer a question and explore possibilities, it doesn't mean they're advocating it.

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: In the past, people have asked me off-the-wall questions about Neanderthals, for example, and then it was described as if I were enthusiastic about it.

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: So, not enthusiastic about NIH and NSF budgets being cut. You could say, well, it forces us to think more seriously about philanthropy and industrial-sponsored research.

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: That could be a positive thing. It could be that that makes us listen more carefully to what society actually needs, rather than doing basic research.

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: I'm a big proponent of basic research, but also maybe I'm more than average connecting the basic research to societal needs from the get-go. I don't think it actually interferes...

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: It's basic research to think and act on societal needs at the same time. So that would be, that could be a positive. It could be that it, it creates a another nation state that now is the dominant force. You know, like China could now become the next empire after...

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Right. Is this the positive story?

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: Well, it could be for China, right? I mean, you didn't specify who it's a positive story for. You know, the US displaced Britain, which displaced, you know, Spain and Portugal, you know, it keeps, keeps moving. Fresh blood is sometimes a good thing. Again, I preface this by saying I'm not advocating this. What else could go well? You know, there's just certain things that we as a society are fairly good at doing collectively that we're not good at doing individually. You know, building roads, schools, and science are examples of that.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: Doesn't mean we couldn't learn how to do that. You know, it, you know, to some extent, when you build a gated community, a lot of that is done with private funding. It's possible we could figure out how to build roads and schools and just about everything. It means we're going to run into some kind of hyper-capitalism that might mean, you know, there's all kinds of pathologies that come along with that.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: What is it about the nature of your work, maybe biology more generally, that makes it possible for one lab to be behind so many advancements? I don't think there's an analogous thing in computer science, which is a field I'm more familiar with, where you could go to one lab and...

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: One academic lab, yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Sorry, one academic lab, and then a hundred different companies have been formed out of it, including the ones that are most exciting and doing a bunch of groundbreaking work. So is it something about the nature of your academic lab? Is it something about the nature of biology research? What explains this pattern?

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: Well, first of all, thank you for being so generous in your evaluation, which maybe take with a grain of salt. But I think what it is is being in the right place at the right time. So Boston is a unique culture. It attracts some of the best and brightest students and postdocs automatically. It has a, it is dense enough, you know, sometimes people want to spread the wealth out evenly all over the universe or the planet. And there's advantages to having it clustered. You know, so like if you have, you know, spouses can, can find other jobs in the same field. So having a concentration of biotech and pharma and MIT and Harvard and BU and so forth, all in one pretty walkable distance, you know, not spread out all along the East or West Coast, but actually...

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: In a walkable city is one thing, that's a starting point. And then a lab that chooses from an early stage to, you know, to keep this dynamic between basic science and societal needs going at all costs, causing great trauma when the lab starts, but then getting a couple of wins and it starts building up a, you know, it's a positive feedback loop where just like the building of Boston was a positive feedback loop, the more Harvards and MITs and high-tech startups and then Pharma. And so you get a couple of wins in the literature and people start coming that are a whole other level up on it, and maybe they're already aiming for entrepreneurship well before they weren't. Anyway, it evolves in a way that you can't just jumpstart. You couldn't just suddenly create Harvard and MIT in the middle of the desert and suddenly create a lab that is taking these kind of risks early in one in a career. And then also the timing is good because the exponential is starting to show up. The exponential is pretty much the same in the beginning of the hockey stick and the end of the hockey stick, but you don't notice it until it gets... and so that's what's happening at both the computing, AI, biotech, they're all peaking at this point. And so whichever lab happened to already have that positive feedback loop going with the academic to industry technology transfer would asymmetrically benefit from that exponential. And to some extent, exponential, you can really look like you're very productive when really you're just kind of sliding downhill. It's like, yeah, look at how productive I am. I just jumped out of a plane and I'm accelerating steadily.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: So yesterday I had a dinner with a bunch of biotech founders and I mentioned that I was going to interview you tomorrow. And so somebody asked, "Wait, how many of the people here have worked in George's lab at some point or worked with him at some point?" And I think 70% or 80% of the people raised their hand. And one of the people suggested, "Oh, you should ask him, how does he spot talent?" Because it is the case that many of the people who are building these leading companies or doing groundbreaking research have been recruited by you, have worked in your lab. So how do you spot talent?

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: Well, I'm glad you framed it as spotting talent. I've heard at least one meme that all you have to do is show up and you'll get in.

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: My lab, which is definitely not true. First of all, there's a lot of self-selection, frankly. Um, we're an acquired taste. You know, technology development is not at all the same skill set as regular biology where you, you know, you pick a gene, you pick a disease, you pick a phenomenon, and you hammer away at it for your whole life. Um, this is more you make a library where you have, you know, a million members of the library are going to fail and maybe one or two will succeed. Um, very different attitude. It's much more engineering, but it's even different from most engineering where, you know, engineering doesn't usually use libraries that way in millions and billions of components that are, you know, non-random, but many of them will fail. Yeah, so your question is selection criteria.

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: Um, so of that, the self-selection and the next thing is in the interview, I typically tell them I'm looking for people that are nice. I'm not necessarily looking for geniuses. We end up with a lot of geniuses. That's wonderful. Um, but nice, I think is highly predictive of how well you will do in the lab and after. And as a consequence, I think we have a, you know, kind of international set of alumni that are quite nice to each other, even though they're supposedly in cutthroat fields. Um, and I think they're nice to other people as well. So that's nice is one criteria. Uh, multidisciplinarity. It's hard to build an multidisciplinary team from disciplinarians. So if you have two people that each know two languages or two skills, even if they don't have anything in common, they have shown that they can learn a new skill and then they'll each add a new skill that connects them as a third thing. So, those are the three main things I would say.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Final question. Given the fast pace of AI progress, your point taken that we should be cautious of this technology, but by default I expect it to go quite fast and there not being some sort of global moratorium on AI progress. Given that's the case, what is the vision for, we're going to have a world with, we're very plausibly have a world with like genuine AGI within the next 20 years. What is the vision for biology given that fact? Because if AI was 100 years away, we could say, well, we've got this research we're doing with the brain or with gene therapies and so forth, which might help us cope or might help us, you know, stay on the same page. Given how fast AI is happening, what is the vision for this bio-AI co-evolution or whatever it might look like?

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: I think one scenario or unlike

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: If if we handle the safety issues and that has to be a top priority if we handle that properly then then we're probably going to have almost perfect health. Uh why why wouldn't we, you know? It's going to go so fast and I mean it's going to go pretty fast with just regular AI without AGI. But if you add to it AGI, then it'll be a positive feedback loop because the more people they get fixed, you know, or get access to good healthcare,

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: the more people will be helping prompt the AI, if that's necessary.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: And I think it probably will be, and the more hybrid systems we'll have of people and and machines working together in harmony.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Hopefully in this very positive scenario, yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: George Church
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.text: Uh well, that's a good vision to end on. George, thank you so much for coming on.

highlights: While natural evolution may take a million years to incorporate a few base pair changes, modern biotechnology enables billions of genetic changes to be performed in a single afternoon.
By 2050, the combination of exponential progress in biotechnology and the development of therapies that reverse aging phenotypes could allow humans to reach a "longevity escape velocity.".
Biotechnology is poised to revolutionize material science, potentially enabling the production of room-temperature superconductors and biological polymers that conduct signals at the speed of light.

Genetic counseling is a significantly under-hyped and cost-effective intervention that could eliminate most rare recessive diseases, allowing gene therapy to be prioritized for more common, age-related conditions.

The increasing accessibility and decreasing cost of biotechnology create urgent biosecurity risks, necessitating a shift toward robust surveillance and whistleblower systems to prevent the accidental or intentional weaponization of synthetic life.
summary:
This transcript features a wide-ranging conversation with renowned biologist George Church regarding the future of longevity, synthetic biology, and biosecurity. Church expresses optimism that "longevity escape velocity" could be achieved by 2050, potentially extending human lifespans to 200 years through somatic gene therapy and "Ship of Theseus" cell replacement, even within the brain. He highlights a shift from focusing on rare genetic diseases to targeting universal age-related phenotypes. Furthermore, he argues that genetic counseling is an under-hyped but critical public health tool, comparable to vaccination, which could effectively eliminate most recessive diseases through better matchmaking and screening.

A major theme of the discussion is the impending biological revolution in material science and manufacturing. Church predicts that by 2040, the convergence of AI and biotechnology will enable "atomic precision" manufacturing, surpassing the limits of current semiconductors. By expanding the genetic code to include non-standard amino acids and the entire periodic table, Church believes we can create biological libraries to discover exotic materials, including room-temperature superconductors. While he is bullish on "scientific AI" for protein and capsid design, he remains cautious regarding Artificial General Intelligence (AGI), viewing it as a potential existential risk that lacks the necessary ethical safeguards.

The dialogue also addresses the ethics and risks of high-level genetic intervention, including de-extinction and biodefense. Church explains that projects like the woolly mammoth are less about "perfect copies" and more about understanding the "minimum" genetic changes required to achieve functional ecological goals. On the darker side of biotech, he warns of "mirror life"—an alternative chirality of life that could be weaponized to wipe out existing biology. He argues that traditional voluntary moratoria are insufficient for biosecurity; instead, the world needs robust surveillance, whistleblower mechanisms, and consequences for bad actors to navigate the democratization of increasingly powerful biotechnologies.
